summary: I started discovering poetry during my childhood in New York City. The environment and the people there gradually influenced my early poetic sensibilities. Later, in the 1950s, I returned to New York and was involved with the Beat poetry scene, which deeply shaped my work.

I moved to California in the late 1940s, and the West Coast alternative scene was very different from New York. This change really shaped my poetic style and artistic direction. Later, during the 1960s, I lived in Sausalito, California, where I was involved in public relations and personal exploration.

In the 1950s, I was deeply involved with the Beat Generation and its poetry scene. Meeting figures like Allen Ginsberg influenced my work, which reflected the energy and struggles of that era. My experiences in places like Brazil and New York also fed into this creative period.

I met Allen Ginsberg during the 1950s as part of my involvement with the Beat poetry scene. His presence and work were significant influences on my own poetry during that time. Our interactions helped me channel the energy and challenges of the Beat Generation into my art.

During the 1950s, I had some experiences in Brazil that influenced my poetry and creative outlook. These moments abroad, combined with my time in New York, enriched my perspective within the Beat Generation. Brazil was part of the broader context that shaped my work then.

In the 1960s, I wrote for Playboy magazine while living on a houseboat in Sausalito. This period was vibrant and full of connections with high society, as well as personal exploration. Writing for Playboy was an important part of my creative and professional life during that decade.

During the 1960s, I lived near San Francisco, specifically on a houseboat in Sausalito. The Bay Area was a hub of cultural and artistic activity that influenced my work in public relations and poetry. San Francisco's vibrant scene was a backdrop to much of my creative exploration then.

I lived on a houseboat in Sausalito, California during the 1960s, which was a unique and inspiring environment. This setting was part of my life while I wrote for Playboy and engaged with public relations and artistic communities. Sausalito offered a blend of personal freedom and creative energy.

My transition into psychedelic multimedia art came through my involvement with USCO. Together, we toured places like Vancouver, Seattle, MIT, and Harvard, blending technology and psychedelia in innovative ways. USCO was central to expanding my artistic expression beyond traditional poetry.

As part of my work with USCO, we took our psychedelic multimedia shows on the road to places including Vancouver. This city was one of several stops where we explored new artistic expressions combining technology and psychedelia. Vancouver was important in bringing our work to diverse audiences.

Seattle was another key city where USCO brought our multimedia psychedelic art performances. Our shows there, along with stops in Vancouver, MIT, and Harvard, helped push the boundaries of art and technology. Seattle's audience was part of this exciting expansion of my creative work.

We brought our USCO psychedelic multimedia art performances to the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, blending technology with creative expression. MIT was a significant venue where we connected with an audience interested in the intersection of art and science. This experience was pivotal in my artistic evolution.

Harvard University was one of the stops on our USCO multimedia art tour, where we showcased psychedelic and technological art forms. Performing there allowed me to engage with an intellectually curious audience and expand the reach of my work. Harvard represented a bridge between traditional academia and avant-garde art.

Michael Callahan was a crucial collaborator in my exploration of technology-driven art. Working with him and Intermedia Systems Corporation allowed me to combine poetry, visuals, and sound in innovative ways. His partnership helped me push the boundaries of multimedia expression.

Intermedia Systems Corporation played an important role in my multimedia art projects, providing technological support and collaboration. Working with them and Michael Callahan enabled me to fuse poetry with visuals and sound in new forms. This partnership was central to my artistic experimentation in the 1960s.

Rabbi Zalman Schachter-Shalomi influenced my spiritual beliefs, which deeply informed my art and life. His teachings, along with Eastern traditions, provided a framework for understanding creativity and ethics. This spiritual grounding was a vital part of my journey as an artist.
